assessment_steps.text: Takes or verbalizes appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Clearly explains CPAP procedure, expected sensations, and importance of keeping mask sealed to the patient
CPAP device with adjustable flow generator
CPAP masks (small, medium, large sizes)
Oxygen source with sufficient supply
Flow meter capable of 10-15 LPM
Pulse oximeter
Waveform capnography (EtCO2) monitor
Bag-valve mask (backup) readily accessible
Assesses patient's respiratory status including respiratory rate, tidal volume, accessory muscle use, and work of breathing
Auscultates lung sounds bilaterally and documents findings
Obtains baseline vital signs including SpO2 and EtCO2
Verifies indication for CPAP use (acute pulmonary edema/CHF, COPD exacerbation, or severe asthma with adequate respiratory effort)

Assesses for and verbalizes contraindications: apnea or near-apnea, active vomiting or inability to protect airway, facial trauma precluding mask seal, suspected pneumothorax, systolic BP less than 90 mmHg
Confirms patient is alert enough to follow commands and maintain own airway
Connects oxygen source to CPAP device and verifies gas flow
Sets initial oxygen flow rate to 10-15 LPM per protocol
Sets initial PEEP to 5 cmH2O (adjustable to 5-10 cmH2O based on patient response)
Selects appropriately sized mask ensuring coverage of nose and mouth without air leak over eyes
Positions patient in high Fowler's or tripod position to optimize respiratory effort
Applies mask to patient's face, starting at bridge of nose, and secures with head straps
Verifies adequate mask seal with no significant air leak around eyes or chin
Coaches patient to breathe normally through the device and not fight the positive pressure
Monitors SpO2 and EtCO2 continuously after application
Reassesses respiratory rate, work of breathing, lung sounds, and mental status every 5 minutes
Adjusts PEEP incrementally (up to 10 cmH2O) if patient remains in distress with adequate blood pressure
Recognizes signs of CPAP failure: worsening respiratory distress, decreasing SpO2, altered mental status, hemodynamic instability, or inability to maintain mask seal
Verbalizes criteria and plan for transitioning to BVM ventilation or advanced airway if CPAP fails
Documents time of application, initial and ongoing vital signs, device settings, and patient response

affective: Interacts with simulated patient and other personnel in professional manner
critical_criteria: Failure to take or verbalize appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Failure to assess patient for contraindications before applying CPAP
Failure to maintain adequate mask seal resulting in ineffective therapy
Failure to continuously monitor patient with SpO2 and EtCO2 after application
Failure to recognize signs of CPAP failure requiring transition to BVM or advanced airway
Failure to have BVM immediately available as backup
Failure to manage the patient as a competent EMT
Exhibits unacceptable affect with patient or other personnel
Uses or orders a dangerous or inappropriate intervention

assessment_steps.text: Takes or verbalizes appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Clearly explains inhaled medication procedure and proper technique to the patient
Metered-dose inhaler (MDI) with spacer device
Small-volume nebulizer kit with mask or mouthpiece
Oxygen source with flow meter
Stethoscope
Prescribed or protocol-authorized medication
Pulse oximeter
Auscultates lung sounds bilaterally before medication administration and documents findings (wheezing, rhonchi, diminished sounds)
Obtains baseline vital signs including respiratory rate, SpO2, and heart rate
Assesses work of breathing including accessory muscle use, speaking in full sentences, and patient positioning
Verifies 5 rights of medication administration: Right Patient, Right Medication, Right Dose, Right Route (inhalation), Right Time
Checks medication for clarity and expiration date
Asks patient about known medication allergies and previous use of inhaled medications
Confirms medication order or standing protocol authorization
For MDI: Shakes inhaler vigorously for 5 seconds to mix medication
For MDI: Primes inhaler if new or not recently used (1-2 test sprays away from patient)
For MDI: Attaches spacer device to inhaler mouthpiece
For MDI: Instructs patient to sit upright, exhale fully, then place spacer mouthpiece in mouth creating seal with lips
For MDI: Coaches patient to begin slow deep inhalation while simultaneously actuating the inhaler canister
For MDI: Instructs patient to hold breath for up to 10 seconds after inhalation, then exhale slowly
For MDI: Waits 1 minute between puffs if multiple doses ordered
For nebulizer: Places prescribed medication dose into nebulizer chamber
For nebulizer: Assembles nebulizer kit connecting T-piece, mouthpiece or mask, and reservoir tubing
For nebulizer: Connects nebulizer to oxygen source and sets flow rate to 6-8 LPM to produce steady mist
For nebulizer: Positions patient upright and instructs patient to breathe normally through mouthpiece with occasional deep breaths
For nebulizer: Instructs patient to hold nebulizer upright and periodically tap chamber to ensure complete medication delivery
For nebulizer: Continues treatment until medication chamber sputters and mist production ceases (approximately 5-10 minutes)
Auscultates lung sounds bilaterally after medication administration and compares to pre-treatment assessment
Reassesses respiratory rate, SpO2, work of breathing, and patient-reported symptom relief
Monitors heart rate for tachycardia and patient for tremors or other side effects of beta-agonist medications
Documents medication administered, dose, route, time, pre- and post-treatment lung sounds, and patient response

affective: Interacts with simulated patient and other personnel in professional manner
critical_criteria: Failure to take or verbalize appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Failure to verify 5 rights of medication administration (right patient, medication, dose, route, time)
Failure to properly assemble MDI with spacer or nebulizer device
Failure to auscultate lung sounds before and after medication administration
Failure to coach patient on proper inhalation technique
Failure to monitor patient for adverse effects after administration
Failure to manage the patient as a competent EMT
Exhibits unacceptable affect with patient or other personnel
Uses or orders a dangerous or inappropriate intervention

assessment_steps.text: Takes or verbalizes appropriate BSI/PPE precautions (gloves at minimum)
Clearly explains blood glucose testing procedure to the patient
Glucometer device
Test strips (compatible with glucometer, not expired)
Lancet device with appropriate depth setting
Alcohol prep swabs
Sterile gauze pads
Sharps container
Control solution for quality verification
Verifies glucometer is functioning and displays within calibration date or verbalizes use of control solution to confirm accuracy
Confirms test strip lot number matches glucometer coding or verifies auto-coding feature is active
Verifies test strips are not expired and container has been properly stored (sealed, not exposed to extreme temperatures)
Selects puncture site on lateral aspect of fingertip (avoids finger pad and thumb/index finger when possible)
Cleanses selected site with alcohol swab using circular motion and allows site to air dry completely
Inserts test strip into glucometer and confirms device is ready for sample
Arms lancet device and applies to prepared site with firm pressure to obtain puncture
Wipes away first drop of blood with sterile gauze, then gently squeezes finger to produce an adequate second drop (avoids excessive squeezing which dilutes sample with interstitial fluid)
Applies blood drop to test strip sample area ensuring adequate fill as indicated by glucometer
Applies direct pressure to puncture site with sterile gauze until bleeding stops
Immediately disposes of used lancet in sharps container without recapping
Reads and verbalizes glucometer result in mg/dL
Interprets result: normal range 70-140 mg/dL, hypoglycemia less than 60 mg/dL, hyperglycemia greater than 300 mg/dL
For critical hypoglycemia (less than 60 mg/dL): verbalizes initiation of treatment per protocol (oral glucose if conscious, IV dextrose or IM glucagon if unconscious)
For critical hyperglycemia (greater than 300 mg/dL): verbalizes assessment for signs of DKA or HHS and appropriate fluid resuscitation
Documents blood glucose value, time of test, site used, and any treatment initiated
Properly disposes of all contaminated materials (test strip, gauze, gloves) in biohazard waste

affective: Interacts with simulated patient and other personnel in professional manner
critical_criteria: Failure to take or verbalize appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Failure to verify glucometer calibration or use control solution
Failure to obtain adequate blood sample for accurate reading
Failure to recognize and verbalize treatment for critical hypoglycemia (less than 60 mg/dL)
Failure to recognize and verbalize treatment for critical hyperglycemia (greater than 300 mg/dL)
Failure to properly dispose of lancet in sharps container
Recaps lancet instead of disposing directly into sharps container
Failure to manage the patient as a competent EMT
Exhibits unacceptable affect with patient or other personnel
Uses or orders a dangerous or inappropriate intervention

assessment_steps.text: Takes or verbalizes appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Clearly explains 12-lead ECG procedure to the patient, including need to expose chest and remain still during acquisition
12-Lead ECG machine (monitor/defibrillator with 12-lead capability)
10 ECG electrodes (4 limb, 6 precordial)
ECG cable set with limb and precordial lead wires
Skin preparation supplies: disposable razor, alcohol prep swabs, abrasive pad or dry gauze
Positions patient supine or semi-reclined with arms at sides and legs uncrossed
Exposes chest completely, maintaining patient dignity with appropriate draping
Identifies electrode placement sites and shaves excessive chest hair at each site if needed to ensure electrode contact
Abrades skin lightly at electrode sites with abrasive pad to remove dead skin cells and reduce impedance
Cleanses each electrode site with alcohol swab and allows to dry completely before electrode placement
Places RA (white) electrode on right arm at bony prominence of distal forearm or right shoulder
Places LA (black) electrode on left arm at bony prominence of distal forearm or left shoulder
Places RL (green) electrode on right leg at bony prominence of distal lower leg or right lower abdomen
Places LL (red) electrode on left leg at bony prominence of distal lower leg or left lower abdomen
Locates angle of Louis (sternal angle) and identifies 4th intercostal space by palpating downward from the 2nd rib
Places V1 (C1) electrode at 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
Places V2 (C2) electrode at 4th intercostal space, left sternal border
Places V4 (C4) electrode at 5th intercostal space, left midclavicular line (placed before V3)
Places V3 (C3) electrode midway between V2 and V4
Places V5 (C5) electrode at anterior axillary line, horizontal to V4
Places V6 (C6) electrode at midaxillary line, horizontal to V4 and V5
Connects all lead wires to corresponding electrodes, verifying correct color-code matching
Instructs patient to remain still, relax arms and legs, and breathe normally during acquisition
Verifies all leads display on monitor with acceptable waveform quality before printing
Troubleshoots artifact if present: checks lead connections, verifies electrode adhesion, instructs patient to relax, applies AC (60 Hz) filter if necessary
Acquires and prints 12-lead ECG tracing with proper standardization (10mm/mV, 25mm/sec)
Verbalizes systematic interpretation: determines rate, rhythm, axis, intervals (PR, QRS, QT), and ST-segment analysis across all 12 leads
Identifies ST-segment elevation greater than 1mm in 2 or more contiguous leads as STEMI criteria
Verbalizes identification of reciprocal ST depression and its significance in confirming acute MI
Initiates electronic or verbal transmission of 12-lead ECG to receiving facility when STEMI or other acute findings are identified
Documents time of acquisition, clinical indication, significant findings, and any transmission to receiving facility
Verbalizes need to obtain serial 12-lead ECGs every 15 minutes or with any change in patient symptoms

affective: Interacts with simulated patient and other personnel in professional manner
critical_criteria: Failure to take or verbalize appropriate BSI/PPE precautions
Failure to correctly identify and place precordial leads V1 through V6
Failure to properly prepare skin resulting in inadequate electrode contact
Failure to obtain artifact-free ECG tracing suitable for interpretation
Failure to recognize STEMI criteria (ST elevation greater than 1mm in 2 or more contiguous leads)
Failure to transmit 12-lead ECG to receiving facility when acute findings are identified
Failure to manage the patient as a competent EMT
Exhibits unacceptable affect with patient or other personnel
Uses or orders a dangerous or inappropriate intervention
